perturbation.original_text: Under this e-business Hosting Agreement ("Agreement") between International Business Machines Corporation ("IBM") and Bluefly, Inc. ("Customer"), IBM will provide Web hosting and related services ("Services") to Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: Under this e-business Hosting Agreement ("Agreement") between International Business Machines Corporation ("IBM") and Bluefly, Inc. ("Client"), IBM will provide Web hosting and related services ("Solutions") to Client.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Customer' to 'Client' and 'Services' to 'Solutions' creates confusion regarding the obligations and rights of each party. Throughout the agreement, both the original terms, 'Customer' and 'Services,' and the new terms, 'Client' and 'Solutions,' are used, leading to uncertainty about which entities and deliverables are being referenced in specific clauses. This contradiction is particularly problematic in sections detailing payment obligations, service level agreements, and termination clauses, where it is unclear whether the conditions apply to 'Client' or 'Customer' or both, and whether the subject matter is 'Services' or 'Solutions.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Customer or IBM may terminate this Agreement for material breach of this Agreement by the other upon written notice containing the specific nature and dates of the material breach. The breaching party will have thirty (30) days from receipt of notice to cure such breach, except for nonpayment by Customer, which must be cured within seven (7) business days from receipt of notice. If such breach has not been timely cured, then the non-breaching party may immediately terminate this Agreement upon written notice; provided, however, it is understood that in the event IBM has so breached this Agreement IBM shall not be entitled to recover the early termination charges described in Section 3.4(b) below.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Customer' to 'Client' and 'Services' to 'Solutions' introduces misaligned terminology, which creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text specifically refers to 'Customer' and outlines termination conditions based on actions by 'Customer.' If the contract now uses both 'Customer' and 'Client' interchangeably, it becomes unclear which entity is subject to the termination clause, thus creating ambiguity and uncertainty about contract enforcement. Similarly, the use of both 'Services' and 'Solutions' in the agreement creates uncertainty about what deliverables and obligations are subject to breach and subsequent termination. This aligns with the provided definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' because the multiple terms are used interchangeably without clear definition, leading to conflicting obligations and uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a conflict between the Base Terms and an attachment, the Base Terms will govern, except where an attachment or a provision contained therein expressly states that it will govern over the Base Terms. The Base Terms and the attachments always govern over any inconsistent provision in an Order Form.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a conflict between the Main Terms and an attachment, the Main Terms will govern, except where an attachment or a provision contained therein expressly states that it will govern over the Main Terms. The Base Terms and the attachments always govern over any inconsistent provision in an Order Form.
perturbation.explanation: Here, the term 'Base Terms' is changed to 'Main Terms' in the first sentence, while the original 'Base Terms' is retained in the second sentence. This introduces an in-text contradiction regarding the governing terms of the agreement. It is unclear whether 'Base Terms' and 'Main Terms' are interchangeable, potentially leading to disputes about which set of terms takes precedence in case of a conflict. This confusion is relevant when determining compliance, resolving disputes, and interpreting the overall obligations of the involved parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.0 Definitions a. "Acceptable Use Policy" means the Acceptable Use Policy for IBM e-business Services, located on the Internet at http://www.ibm.com/services/ e-business/aup.html, as of the Effective Date, and any subsequent modification in accordance with Section 13.2 below. b. "Affiliates" means entities that control, are controlled by, or are under common control with a party to this Agreement. c. "Base Components" means the hardware and software that IBM makes available, if any, as specified in Order Forms and associated Service Option Attachments. d. "Content" means information, software, and data that Customer provides, including, without limitation, any hypertext markup language files, scripts, programs, recordings, sound, music, graphics, images, applets or servlets that Customer or its Subcontractors or Services Recipients create, install, upload or transfer in or through the e-business Hosting Environment and/or Customer Components. e. "Content Administrator" means an employee or Subcontractor of Customer who is authorized by Customer to install, upload and/or maintain Content using a User Identification. f. "Customer Components" means the hardware, software and other products, data and Content that Customer provides, including those specified in Service Option Attachments. g. "e-business Hosting Environment" means the Base Components and the IBM provided Internet access bandwidth, collectively. h. "Enterprise" means any legal entity and the subsidiaries it owns by more than 50 percent. The term "Enterprise" applies only to the portion of the Enterprise physically located within the United States of America. i. "IBM e-business Hosting Center" means the facility used by IBM to provide the Services. j. "Internet" means the public worldwide network of TCP/IP-based networks. k. "Materials" means literary or other works of authorship (such as programs, program listings, programming tools, documentation, reports, drawings and similar works) that IBM may deliver to Customer. "Materials" does not include licensed program products available under their own license agreements or Base Components. l. "Required Consents" means any consents or approvals required to give IBM and its Subcontractors the right or license to access, use and/or modify in electronic form and in other forms, including derivative works, the Customer Components, without infringing the ownership or intellectual property rights of the providers, licensors, or owners of such Customer Components. m. "Service Option Ready Date" means the date that IBM has notified Customer that IBM has completed the implementation activities 2 specified in an applicable Service Option Attachment. n. "Services Recipients" means any entities or individuals receiving or using the Services, or the results or products of the Services. o. "Service Option Attachment Start Date" means the day after the date of the last signature on an Order Form authorizing the Services under an applicable Service Option Attachment. p. "Subcontractor" means a contractor, vendor, agent, or consultant selected and retained by IBM or Customer, respectively. q. "TCP/IP" means Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol. r. "User Identification" or "ID" means a string of characters that uniquely identifies a Content Administrator.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Base Terms' with 'Main Terms' in the first sentence of the section while retaining 'Base Terms' in the second sentence. The contradicted text defines 'Base Components.' If 'Base Terms' and 'Main Terms' are not interchangeable, the second sentence of the changed text creates an in-text contradiction because it references 'Base Terms' which is now undefined in the immediate context and also causes confusion on if 'Base Terms' and 'Base Components' are related terms.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Termination for Cause Customer or IBM may terminate this Agreement for material breach of this Agreement by the other upon written notice containing the specific nature and dates of the material breach. The breaching party will have thirty (30) days from receipt of notice to cure such breach, except for nonpayment by Customer, which must be cured within seven (7) business days from receipt of notice. If such breach has not been timely cured, then the non-breaching party may immediately terminate this Agreement upon written notice; provided, however, it is understood that in the event IBM has so breached this Agreement IBM shall not be entitled to recover the early termination charges described in Section 3.4(b) below.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Termination for Cause Customer or IBM may terminate this Agreement for material breach of this Agreement by the other upon written notice containing the specific nature and dates of the material breach. The breaching party will have thirty (30) days from receipt of notice to cure such breach, except for nonpayment by End-User, which must be cured within seven (7) business days from receipt of notice. If such breach has not been timely cured, then the non-breaching party may immediately terminate this Agreement upon written notice; provided, however, it is understood that in the event IBM has so breached this Agreement IBM shall not be entitled to recover the early termination charges described in Section 3.4(b) below.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Customer' to 'End-User' within the same section introduces ambiguity. Throughout the contract, the initial designation 'Customer' is used to define Bluefly, Inc., and suddenly introducing 'End-User' without a clear definition leads to confusion about which party is responsible for payments and subject to termination clauses. This is crucial because it affects the enforceability of the termination rights and obligations outlined in the section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due upon receipt and payable as specified in such invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Customer' to 'End-User' creates a contradiction. Section 4.2 explicitly states that 'Customer agrees to pay accordingly'. By changing 'Customer' to 'End-User' in Section 3.3, it introduces ambiguity as to who is responsible for payment, directly conflicting with the clarity provided in Section 4.2. This introduces uncertainty about who is bound by the payment obligations, leading to a clear contradiction and making the contract unenforceable as written.
